result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My father never fixed it and my mother never asked him to, and I think that tells you almost everything about their marriage that you need to know.

I've been thinking about the things we leave broken. Not because we can't fix them. Because fixing them would mean admitting they're broken, and that's a different kind of work entirely. It's easier to walk past the radio every morning and pour your coffee and pretend the silence is something you chose.

I do this too. I've left things broken in myself that I walk past so regularly I've stopped seeing them. A door I don't open. A conversation I've been meaning to have since 2019. A version of myself I put down somewhere and haven't gone back for.

---

There's a particular kind of afternoon that only happens in late October. The light goes yellow and thin, and the shadows get long before you're ready for them. Everything looks like a photograph of itself rather than the thing itself. I don't know the science of it. I know it makes me feel like I'm standing slightly outside my own life, watching it happen through glass.

I felt that way as a kid and I thought I would grow out of it. I didn't. I just got better at naming it, which is not the same as getting over it.

---

Someone told me once that nostalgia is a form of grief. That you can only feel nostalgic for something you know you can't return to, which means nostalgia is just the emotional acknowledgment of time being a one-way door.

I pushed back on that at the time. I said I thought nostalgia could be warm, could be good, could be something you'd want. And the person said yes, that's exactly what makes it grief. The warmth. The wanting.

I've been thinking about that for three years. I think they were right.

---

The specific texture of certain memories: the backseat of a car on a long drive at night, the highway lights making orange intervals of your parents' faces in the rearview mirror. The particular weight of falling asleep somewhere you're not supposed to fall asleep and being carried. Waking up in your own bed and not knowing how you got there and it being the safest feeling in the world.

Nobody carries you anymore. At some point that just stops and nobody marks the last time it happens, which means you don't know to pay attention, which means you don't remember it. The last time someone carried you is lost. I think about that.

---

I want to write something true before I stop. Not performatively true. Not the kind of true that's aware of being read.

Here: I am afraid of becoming someone who stopped trying. I know people like that. They got tired or they got hurt or they just ran out of a particular kind of energy that nobody tells you is finite until it is. And something behind their eyes went quiet. And they made their peace with less. And from the outside it looks almost like contentment.

I'm not sure I would make my peace with it. I'm not sure I'd know the difference.

---

The broken radio. My father died eight years ago and my mother still has it. I went home last Christmas and it was still on the counter, still broken, and I almost asked her about it. Why she keeps it. What it means to her now.

I didn't ask. I poured my coffee and stood in that kitchen and looked at it and the silence felt like something. I can't tell you what exactly.

Maybe that's okay. Maybe not everything needs to be said out loud to be real. Maybe some things live in the space between the question and the asking, in the broken radio that stays on the counter for eleven years after the man who should have fixed it is gone.

Maybe the radio was never about the radio.

I don't know. I've got a few words left and no clean ending for this, which feels honest at least. Things don't end clean. They just go quiet, and you learn to listen to the static, and sometimes the static is enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
